---
title: "LRPITT12: Joint ITT analysis across the suite outcomes"

date: last-modified
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm:ss Z"

format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
    theme: cosmo
    number-sections: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-links:
      - repo
---

::: {.callout-note}
Drafted by a LLM-based AI tool (Codex/GPT-5).
:::

{{< include _partials/_header.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_setup.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_gate_badge.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_key_findings.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_reading_guide.qmd >}}

## Overview

LRPITT12 fits all baseline-bearing LRPITT outcomes in one PyMC graph using the randomised phase only. Because residual correlation is disabled and the priors are independent by outcome, the posterior factorises into a product of outcome-specific marginals. This is a computationally convenient consistency analysis, not a dependence-aware joint posterior. Cross-outcome comparisons therefore use standardised probability-scale average marginal effects and retain an explicit paired-dependence caveat.

The proximal conditional logit coefficients should track their single-outcome counterparts closely. The distal outcomes (R, E, UR, UE) carry the tiered $\mathrm{Normal}(0, 0.3)$ prior in the single-outcome suite but the untiered $\mathrm{Normal}(0, 0.5)$ prior here, so they may differ by a **prior-driven** amount that is not Monte Carlo noise (and the multi-outcome model is fit on $n = 53$ versus single-outcome $n = 54$).

The model is a product of ten linear Beta-Binomial marginals with common predictor structure: outcome-specific intercepts, treatment effects, own-baseline and linear-age **precision** terms, and outcome-specific dispersion. It has no cross-baselines, age Gaussian process or LKJ residual correlation.

::: {.callout-note title="Outcome set: ten of the eleven suite measures"}

The joint covers the **baseline-bearing** outcomes
$(\tau_{TR}, \tau_{TE}, \tau_{UR}, \tau_{UE}, \tau_R, \tau_E, \tau_L, \tau_B, \tau_P, \tau_W)$.

- **Nonword (N) is excluded**: it is post-only and its degenerate baseline cannot
  enter the joint's dense pre-score matrix. Its treatment effect is read from the
  single-outcome **off-floor** model LRPITT11.
- **Phonetic spelling (P) is included as a graded outcome** (its floored baseline
  simply shrinks $\gamma^{\text{own}}_P$); the binary off-floor exploratory analysis for P is in
  LRPITT09. The joint $\tau_P$ here is the graded effect — read it alongside the
  off-floor result, not on its own.

:::

## Structure

For child $i$ and outcome $k$:

$$
\eta_{k,i} = \alpha_k + \tau_k G_i
           + \gamma^{\text{own}}_k\, \text{logit}_*(k_{\text{pre},i}, N_k)
           + \gamma^{A}_k\, A_{\text{std},i},
$$

with a per-outcome Beta-Binomial likelihood, $\mu_{k,i} = \sigma(\eta_{k,i})$, and
outcome-specific dispersion $\kappa_k$. Observations with a missing post-score are
masked per-outcome. **Sign convention:** $G = 1$ is the intervention arm, so a
**positive** $\tau_k$ means the intervention raised outcome $k$.

The LKJ residual-correlation and age-GP flags are off by default because previous fits found them weakly identified or divergence-inducing. Cross-outcome uncertainty must not be described as propagating estimated within-child covariance. PSIS-LOO is aggregated over observed outcome cells within child, so its predictive unit is a held-out child rather than a held-out child-outcome cell.

![Model diagram](model_graph.png){#fig-model .lightbox fig-align="left"}

{{< include _partials/_results_joint.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_priors.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_prior_predictive.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_technical.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_footer.qmd >}}
